questions.question: Describe the primary function of a consensus mechanism within a blockchain and name two common examples.
keyPoints: Ensures all network participants agree on the validity of transactions and the state of the ledger.
Prevents double-spending and maintains network security without a central authority.
Examples include Proof of Work (e.g., Bitcoin) and Proof of Stake (e.g., Ethereum).

questions.question: Explain the role and importance of cryptographic hashing in the structure of a blockchain.
keyPoints: Creates a unique digital fingerprint (hash) for each block of data, ensuring data integrity.
Connects blocks chronologically; each block contains the hash of the previous block, forming an immutable chain.
Makes the ledger tamper-evident; altering any data in a past block would change its hash and break the chain.

questions.question: Detail the process of how a transaction is added to a blockchain, from initiation to confirmation, and explain how this process achieves decentralization and trustlessness.
keyPoints: A user initiates a transaction, which is cryptographically signed and broadcast to the peer-to-peer network.
Network nodes (miners/validators) collect transactions into a candidate block and compete to validate it via the consensus mechanism (e.g., solving a PoW puzzle).
The validated block is broadcast to the network, where nodes verify its validity and append it to their copy of the chain, updating the distributed ledger.
Decentralization is achieved as no single entity controls the ledger; trustlessness is achieved through cryptographic proof and consensus rules, eliminating the need for trusted intermediaries.
